questions.question: Explain the purpose and usage of sigpending() function in Unix signal handling.
keyPoints: sigpending() examines pending signals that have been generated but blocked
It takes pointer to sigset_t as parameter
Returns 0 on success, -1 on error
Useful for checking which blocked signals have arrived

questions.question: What is the difference between a blocked signal and a pending signal? Give an example scenario.
keyPoints: Blocked signals are in the signal mask (prevented from delivery)
Pending signals are generated but held waiting for delivery
A signal becomes pending when generated while blocked
Example: SIGINT blocked → signal arrives → becomes pending → unblock → delivered

questions.question: Write the steps to check if SIGTERM is currently pending in a process.
keyPoints: Declare sigset_t pending_set
Initialize with sigemptyset()
Call sigpending(&pending_set)
Use sigismember(&pending_set, SIGTERM) to check
Returns 1 if pending, 0 if not

questions.question: Explain sigset_t data type and four functions used to manipulate signal sets.
keyPoints: sigset_t is an opaque data type for representing signal sets
sigemptyset() - initializes empty set
sigfillset() - initializes set with all signals
sigaddset() - adds signal to set
sigdelset() - removes signal from set
sigismember() - checks if signal is in set

questions.question: How does sigpending() help in implementing race-free signal handling?
keyPoints: Allows checking pending signals before changing mask
Prevents losing signals between check and unblock
Can be used in a loop to handle all pending signals
Useful with sigprocmask() for atomic operations
Enables handling signals without using signal handlers

questions.question: What happens when multiple instances of the same signal are generated while blocked?
keyPoints: For standard signals, only one instance is kept pending
Multiple occurrences are merged into one
Real-time signals (SIGRTMIN-SIGRTMAX) support queuing
sigpending() indicates the signal type is pending
The pending flag is set regardless of count

questions.question: Differentiate between sigpending() and sigprocmask() functions.
keyPoints: sigpending() examines/queries pending signals
sigprocmask() changes/modifies the signal mask
sigpending() is read-only operation
sigprocmask() can block or unblock signals
Both take sigset_t parameter but different purposes

questions.question: Write a C program that blocks SIGUSR1, generates it, and then uses sigpending() to verify it is pending before unblocking and handling it.
keyPoints: Include <signal.h> and necessary headers
Define signal handler using sigaction
Block SIGUSR1 using sigprocmask with SIG_BLOCK
Generate SIGUSR1 using raise() or kill()
Use sigpending() to check pending signals
Unblock SIGUSR1 using sigprocmask with SIG_UNBLOCK
Signal gets delivered to handler
Compile and run to demonstrate

questions.question: Explain the relationship between signal generation, delivery, and pending state. Include how sigprocmask() and sigpending() interact in this process.
keyPoints: Signal generation creates signal (kernel marks it)
Signal mask determines if signal is blocked
If blocked: signal becomes PENDING (stored in pending set)
If not blocked: signal is DELIVERED (handler runs or default action)
sigprocmask() changes the mask (blocks/unblocks)
sigpending() queries which signals are pending
When blocked signal is unblocked, pending signals are delivered
Process of handling: Generate → Mask Check → Pending Queue → Unblock → Deliver

questions.question: Design a signal handling mechanism where a process waits for a specific blocked signal (SIGUSR1) to become pending, then processes it. Explain using sigpending() and sigprocmask().
keyPoints: Block SIGUSR1 initially using sigprocmask
Use sigpending() in a loop or select() to wait
When sigpending() shows SIGUSR1 pending, proceed
Unblock SIGUSR1 using sigprocmask or use sigsuspend()
Process the signal (call handler or take action
Use sigemptyset/sigaddset for signal sets
Ensure race conditions avoided
May use SA_SIGINFO for additional info
